parents.father_name: Hugh Thompson of East Windsor CT (per EH 1909 #529)
parents.father_research_notes: Per Edward Hooker 1909 #529: 'm. 1798 [sic — likely 1788], Anna Thompson, daughter of Hugh Thompson of East Windsor, Conn.' EH date '1798' may be transcription error; her 1786 first child suggests marriage 1785-86. Hugh Thompson East Windsor specific parentage not yet built.

narrative_blocks.header: Died age forty-two leaving seven children
narrative_blocks.body: Died Westhampton MA April 2, 1810 at age forty-two — leaving seven children ranging from twenty-four-year-old Henry down to eight-year-old Edward. Her son middle-named for her father Hugh Thompson (Hugh Thompson Hooker, born 1789) carries the East Windsor Connecticut Thompson family name forward.

parents.father_name: Hugh Thompson of East Windsor CT (per EH 1909 #529)
parents.father_research_notes: Per Edward Hooker 1909 #529: 'm. 1798 [sic — likely 1788], Anna Thompson, daughter of Hugh Thompson of East Windsor, Conn.' EH date '1798' may be transcription error; her 1786 first child suggests marriage 1785-86. Hugh Thompson East Windsor specific parentage not yet built.

narrative_blocks.header: Died age forty-two leaving seven children
narrative_blocks.body: Died Westhampton MA April 2, 1810 at age forty-two — leaving seven children ranging from twenty-four-year-old Henry down to eight-year-old Edward. Her son middle-named for her father Hugh Thompson (Hugh Thompson Hooker, born 1789) carries the East Windsor Connecticut Thompson family name forward.

career.notes: Practiced medicine at Westhampton MA per EH 1909 #529. Established the Hooker physician tradition in Westhampton continued by son Dr. Anson Hooker (H01414, 1799-1869) and through several Westhampton descendants.
Per EH 1909 #529: 'He was a physician and also a trader at West Hampton, Mass.' — supplementing physician income with a trading operation, common pattern for rural New England doctors of the period.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived to ninety-four as Westhampton physician
narrative_blocks.body: Born Northampton MA 1766; died Westhampton MA February 27, 1861 at age ninety-four — extraordinary longevity spanning the early Republic through the Civil War's outbreak. Practiced medicine in Westhampton MA and also operated as a trader; both occupations supported a large family of seven children with first wife Anna Thompson before her 1810 death.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost wife at forty-two, remarried after two years
narrative_blocks.body: First wife Anna Thompson died Westhampton April 2, 1810 at age forty-two — leaving him with seven children ranging from twenty-four-year-old Henry down to eight-year-old Edward. William remarried 1812 to Rhoda Phelps, widow of Ensign Hartland of Hartland Connecticut. No documented children from the second marriage; Rhoda predeceased William by fourteen years.

military_service.war: Likely War of 1812 (age 26 at outbreak; rank Capt confirmed)
military_service.notes: Findagrave Memorial #122605184 carries 'VETERAN' marker. Captain rank confirmed in displayed name. Born 1786 — would have been 26 at outbreak of War of 1812. Specific unit + service dates not yet documented.

narrative_blocks.header: Married at seventeen, first child at eighteen
narrative_blocks.body: Married Sylvia Wales 1803 in Westhampton when both were seventeen years old. Their first child Emily Hooker Kingsley was born May 1804 when Henry was eighteen. Five children followed over seventeen years, the last (Almira Hooker, born 1820) carrying the name of Henry's young-deceased sister Almira Hooker Hale who had died age twenty-four in 1816.

parents.father_research_notes: Wales family of Westhampton MA. Specific parents not identified.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband died on daughter Ellen's thirty-fourth birthday
narrative_blocks.body: Orrin Kingsley died June 6, 1876 at Westhampton — the thirty-fourth birthday of his youngest daughter Ellen Maria, born June 6, 1842 at Northampton. Emily had predeceased him by five years, dying April 17, 1871 at Westhampton.

narrative_blocks.header: Named son Charles Hooker, preserving birth surname
narrative_blocks.body: Among her six Kingsley children, Emily gave the fourth the middle name Hooker — placing her birth surname in the middle position so the family identity would persist into the next Kingsley generation. The child died young; the Hooker name did not carry forward in the surviving siblings.

narrative_blocks.header: Adopted Boston-born Louisa, who died at three
narrative_blocks.body: Emily and Orrin Kingsley adopted a Boston-born child, Louisa W. Kingsley, who came to Westhampton sometime before November 1847. She died there three years after her 1844 birth; her original family surname appears as Somerby on FindAGrave records.

bio.bio_blurb: Westhampton farmer of the Hooker line

narrative_blocks.header: Buried infant Worthington; named next son Worthington
narrative_blocks.body: Festus named his fifth child Worthington in April 1846, but the infant died December 10 of that year at eight months. His sixth son, born November 1848, was named Worthington again — the replacement naming common in the era, and a pattern repeated in the same Hooker generation when his uncle Henry named a daughter Almira after a deceased sibling.

narrative_blocks.header: He gave the lost son’s name again
narrative_blocks.body: When his infant son Worthington died, the Westhampton farmer gave the name again to his next boy — a quiet act of remembrance carried in the family register.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Anson Hooker, he survived just nine days
narrative_blocks.body: Electa and Anson Chapman's third child was given the name Anson Hooker Chapman — combining the father's first name with the mother's birth surname. He was born July 14, 1838 and buried July 23, nine days later; the Hooker surname did not survive in the Chapman line.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for her father Henry's sister Almira Hooker Hale, who died at Westhampton in 1816 at age twenty-three, four years before this Almira's birth.

parents.father_research_notes: Dr. William Hooker (H00529, 1766-?), physician + trader at West Hampton MA; born Northampton MA Nov 26, 1766; son of Rev John and Sarah Worthington Hooker of Northampton. Per EH 1909 #529: 'William Hooker (John* John* John,3 Samuel,2 Thomas1)...m. 1798 Anna Thompson, daughter of Hugh Thompson of East Windsor CT. She died at West Hampton MA. He married No. 2, Widow of Ensign Hartland.'
parents.mother_research_notes: Anna Thompson Hooker not yet in tree.

career.location: West Hampton, Norwich, and Westfield Massachusetts
career.notes: Merchant at West Hampton MA, then Norwich MA. In 1819 removed family to Westfield MA, where they remained.

narrative_blocks.header: Moved family from Norwich Massachusetts to Westfield, 1819
narrative_blocks.body: Merchant at West Hampton MA before relocating to Norwich MA where his two eldest children Lucy Ashmun and William Throop were born. In 1819 he moved the family to Westfield MA, where son Edward was born 1822 and where William and his wife Octavia would both be buried at Pine Hill Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Lucy Ashmun died at Rushville Illinois, 1839
narrative_blocks.body: His eldest child Lucy Ashmun died age 26 at Rushville IL April 17, 1839 — only seventeen months after her Massachusetts marriage to Dr Joseph Washburn Clark and five weeks after the birth of their infant son who himself died in May. The Illinois frontier had taken his daughter, son-in-law's first family, and grandson all within a year.

parents.mother_research_notes: Octavia Throop Hale (1754-1839) of Bozrah CT, died Westhampton MA aged 85. Not yet built (deferred).

cross_connections.display_label: Uncle executed as Continental Army spy, September 1776

narrative_blocks.header: Niece of Nathan Hale the patriot martyr
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Rev Enoch Hale of Westhampton MA was elder brother of Capt Nathan Hale — the Continental Army officer hanged as spy by the British at New York September 22, 1776. Nathan died nearly twelve years before her birth; the family memory of the executed uncle shaped her own brother's name (Nathan Hale Sr 1784-1863) and reached into her children's generation through her son's cousin marriage to her sister Sybella Hale Hall's daughter Sarah.

narrative_blocks.header: Died of dementia paralytica at Northampton, 1867
narrative_blocks.body: Died December 21, 1867 at Northampton MA age 79 — eighteen months after her husband — with cause given on her death record as 'Dementile Paralysis,' the period name for dementia paralytica, the late-stage neurological syndrome of progressive cognitive and motor decline.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at Rushville Illinois eighteen months after marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Married Dr Joseph Washburn Clark of Rushville IL on her twenty-fifth birthday September 28, 1837 and moved with him to the Illinois frontier. Eighteen months later — five weeks after the April 12 birth of their only child William Hooker Clark — she died at Rushville on April 17, 1839. The infant followed her on May 6, 1839.

death.notes: Exact death date not in Sam-supplied sources.

career.organization: Farmers and Mechanics Bank of Hartford CT; then Continental Bank of New York City
career.location: Hartford CT, then New York City
career.notes: Cashier of the Farmers and Mechanics Bank of Hartford CT; later cashier of the Continental Bank of New York City.

narrative_blocks.header: Bank cashier in Hartford and New York City
narrative_blocks.body: Cashier of the Farmers and Mechanics Bank of Hartford CT, then cashier of the Continental Bank of New York City. Three of his six children — Henry Collins, William Augustus, and Caroline Christine — were born at Hartford while he held the Farmers and Mechanics post.

birth.notes: FindAGrave gives Westfield (family moved 1819 per EH H01410); EH 1909 has 'West hampton' likely a typo or family-origin reference. Using Westfield as more consistent with timeline.

death.cause: Mitral regurgitation of the heart (per death record)

career.organization: Phoenix Bank Hartford CT; Great Barrington bank; Westfield Bank
career.notes: Started as clerk in Phoenix Bank Hartford CT 1836 age 15; remained connected with that bank until 1847. Cashier of bank at Great Barrington MA 1847. Cashier of Westfield Bank, Westfield MA from 1851.

cross_connections.display_label: Mother of his wife Sarah Hall; his aunt through the Hale family
His uncle through the Hale family; married two Hooker women

narrative_blocks.header: Married his first cousin Sarah Hall, 1844
narrative_blocks.body: Married Sarah Hall (1819-1898) of Greenfield MA on October 10, 1844. Sarah's mother Sibylla Hale Hall was his own mother Octavia's sister — both daughters of Rev Enoch Hale of Westhampton — making the bride and groom first cousins and the marriage a second Hale-Hooker knot in the same generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Bank clerk at fifteen, Westfield Bank cashier
narrative_blocks.body: Became a clerk in the Phoenix Bank at Hartford CT in 1836 at age fifteen — staying eleven years before moving to Great Barrington MA as bank cashier in 1847. From 1851 he was cashier of the Westfield Bank in Westfield MA, where he remained until his death June 13, 1896 age 75.

death.notes: NOT TO BE CONFUSED with the unrelated 19th-c Commander Edward Hooker, US Navy.

career.notes: Banker in New York City until 1859.

narrative_blocks.header: New York City banker until 1859
narrative_blocks.body: Born Westfield MA the year after the family's 1819 move from Norwich MA, youngest of the four William Hooker Jr / Octavia Hale Hooker children. Married Georgeanna C. Loring December 22, 1852 and was a banker in New York City until 1859 — joining his elder brother William Throop in the city's banking world before leaving Wall Street near the panic year.

burial.plot_notes: Buried at his wife Georgiana's family cemetery — she was born Cincinnati OH 1827.

career.notes: Per EH 1909 #1411: 'He was a trader and farmer at West Hampton, Mass.'

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Thompson honors maternal grandfather Hugh Thompson of East Windsor CT, father of Anna Thompson Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Children died at twenty and twenty-two; outlived both
narrative_blocks.body: Worthington died August 1, 1843 at age twenty, and his sister Sarah Judd died February 6, 1848 at age twenty-two — both before their father, within five years of each other. Hugh Thompson outlived them both by a decade, dying at Westhampton April 20, 1856; his wife Sarah Judd Hooker followed seven years later.

parents.father_research_notes: Sylvester Judd of West Hampton MA per EH 1909 #1411.

bio.bio_blurb: Hooker daughter who married Dr. Enoch Hale

burial.plot_notes: Gravestone inscription: 'Almira, only Daughter of Dctr. William and Mrs. Anna Hooker, was born at Westhampton Nov. 10th 1792. She was married to Doctr. Enoch Hale Sept. 6th 1813 and died at Gardiner Dec. 5th 1816.'

cross_connections.display_label: First cousin Sarah Hooker became Enoch's second wife, 1822

narrative_blocks.header: Dead at twenty-three; niece bore her name
narrative_blocks.body: In September 1813, Almira married Enoch Hale, a physician and son of West Hampton's first minister — the same year her brother William had married Enoch's sister Octavia, binding two Hooker children and two Hale siblings in the same Westhampton community. Almira died at Gardiner Maine on December 5, 1816 at age twenty-four, only three years into the marriage.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Hookers married two Hales
narrative_blocks.body: In neighboring years a Hooker brother and sister each married a Hale: her brother William wed Octavia Hale in 1811, and she married Octavia’s brother, Dr. Enoch Hale, in 1813 — a double knot between two Westhampton families.

parents.father_research_notes: Rev. Enoch Hale (X02859), first minister of West Hampton MA, elder brother of Nathan Hale per EH 1909 #1410.
parents.mother_research_notes: Octavia Throop Hale — daughter of Rev. Enoch and Octavia Throop Hale of Bozrah CT per EH 1909 #1410.

career.notes: Per EH 1909 #1412: 'Enoch Hale, M.D.' Son of Rev. Enoch Hale, first minister of West Hampton.

cross_connections.display_label: Uncle; Continental Army spy executed by British, 1776
His nephew Henry Hooker married a Hale first cousin, 1844

narrative_blocks.header: Married two Hooker first cousins in sequence
narrative_blocks.body: After his first wife Almira Hooker died at Gardiner Maine in December 1816, Dr. Enoch Hale married Sarah Hooker in 1822 — Almira's own first cousin, daughter of Judge John Hooker of Springfield. Sarah died three years later in 1825; Enoch's third wife Jane Murdock, whom he married in 1827, survived him by forty-three years.

narrative_blocks.header: Nathan Hale's execution shadowed his Westhampton childhood
narrative_blocks.body: His father Rev. Enoch Hale was the elder brother of Capt. Nathan Hale, the Continental Army officer executed as a spy by the British in September 1776. Dr. Enoch Hale grew up in a household that lived under the shadow of his famous uncle's martyrdom.

marriages.notes: EH records unnamed infant (b. and d. 1832) by this marriage — non-standard EH ID '2760a'; no JSON entry created pending compiler decision on alphanumeric IDs.

career.notes: Per EH 1909: 'not only became noted as a physician but was also prominent in the public affairs of the place.'
Representative of East Cambridge in the Massachusetts General Court per EH 1909.

cross_connections.display_label: Studied medicine at East Cambridge; died of hospital disease, 1858
Son who joined his practice; died New Year's Eve 1873

narrative_blocks.header: Nephew died six weeks after son's birth, 1858
narrative_blocks.body: His nephew Edward Hooker came to East Cambridge to study medicine after graduating Williams College in 1855, but died April 26, 1858 of disease contracted in hospital service — six weeks after Anson's youngest son Edward Dwight was born in the same East Cambridge household on March 17. The same spring brought both a birth and a death into the Hooker physician line.

narrative_blocks.header: Common Council to State House in one career
narrative_blocks.body: Beyond his East Cambridge medical practice, Anson served successively on the Common Council, as Alderman, and as a Representative in the Massachusetts General Court — three ascending levels of civic office held while maintaining his practice from 1822 until his death in 1869. Harvard-trained physicians of his generation were expected to lead their communities as well as treat them.

narrative_blocks.header: First of three consecutive Harvard physician generations, 1822
narrative_blocks.body: Dr. Anson Hooker graduated Harvard Medical School in 1822 and built a Cambridge practice that his son Anson Parker inherited after Harvard M.D. 1855, and his grandson Edward Dwight carried on after Harvard M.D. 1884. Three consecutive Hooker physicians trained at Harvard across sixty-two years.

parents.father_research_notes: Samuel Parker of East Cambridge MA per EH 1909 #1414.
Per EH 1909 #1414: 'Anna Wright of Cambridge, Mass.'

notable.notable_blurb: surgeon, 26th Massachusetts Infantry, Department of the Gulf
notable.primary_url_label: Obituary, New England Journal of Medicine, January 8, 1874

education.notes: Harvard Medical School M.D. 1855 per EH 1909 #2760.

career.notes: Per EH: 'connected with his father in practice in East Cambridge, and after his father's death succeeded to a large practice.'

military_service.notes: Commissioned surgeon September 10, 1861. Served in Department of the Gulf (Ship Island, Mississippi, then New Orleans area following its capture April 1862). Resigned June 18, 1862 due to disability from exhausting tropical diseases. First of young surgeons to offer services to Governor John Andrew at the start of the war per NEJM obituary January 8, 1874. Returned to state service in charge of military camps, May 1863. Tribute from Surgeon General Dale at his passing.

institutions.institution_blurb: Harvard College A.B. 1851 and Harvard Medical School M.D. 1855.

cross_connections.display_label: Father and East Cambridge physician partner before his death

narrative_blocks.header: Succeeded father's practice; died New Year's Eve 1873
narrative_blocks.body: After Harvard College 1851 and Harvard Medical School 1855, Anson Parker joined his father Dr. Anson Hooker in practice at East Cambridge — the third consecutive Hooker physician in the Westhampton-to-Cambridge line. He died December 31, 1873 at forty-four, New Year's Eve, leaving a large practice he had held only since his father's 1869 death.

narrative_blocks.header: One twin died; one outlived Cotton Mather's descendants
narrative_blocks.body: On March 15, 1866, twin daughters Elizabeth Mather and Mary Marshall were born at East Cambridge; Mary Marshall died four days later. Elizabeth Mather survived and was identified by Edward Hooker in his 1909 genealogy as the last living descendant of Cotton Mather.

narrative_blocks.header: First surgeon to volunteer; disability hastened death
narrative_blocks.body: Anson Parker Hooker was the first of the young surgeons to offer his services to Governor John Andrew at the outbreak of the Civil War, receiving his commission to the 26th Massachusetts on September 10, 1861, and serving in the Department of the Gulf. Exhausting tropical diseases forced his resignation June 18, 1862 — and the resulting disability was cited in his New England Journal of Medicine obituary as having hastened his death at forty-four.

education.notes: Harvard Medical School M.D. 1884 per obituary (previous JSON had 1883 per EH — corrected).

career.notes: Per EH 1909: 'a practising physician at Cambridge, Mass.' as of EH writing.
Per obituary: 'served at New York state hospital' — likely post-M.D. training or staff position before returning to Cambridge/Arlington practice.

institutions.institution_blurb: Harvard College A.B. 1880 and Harvard Medical School M.D. 1884 per obituary.

narrative_blocks.header: Born weeks before his cousin died studying medicine
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Dwight was born at East Cambridge on March 17, 1858, just six weeks before his first cousin Edward Hooker — who had been studying medicine under Anson in that same household — died of disease contracted in hospital service on April 26. That spring, one Hooker physician career began as another ended before it could start.

narrative_blocks.header: Septicemia claimed the best-known Cambridge physician
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Dwight Hooker graduated Harvard College in 1880 and Harvard Medical School in 1884, trained at New York State Hospital, and became one of the best-known physicians in Cambridge and Arlington. He died of septicemia October 27, 1911 at age fifty-two, continuing the Hooker family's Harvard medical tradition into a third generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Three generations of Harvard physicians: 1822, 1855, 1884
narrative_blocks.body: His grandfather Dr. Anson Hooker took his Harvard Medical degree in 1822, his father Dr. Anson Parker Hooker earned a Harvard A.B. in 1851 and returned for his M.D. in 1855, and Edward Dwight completed the arc with a Harvard A.B. in 1880 and M.D. in 1884 — three consecutive Hooker physicians trained at Harvard across sixty-two years. Each carried the Cambridge practice to the next generation.

career.notes: First law practice after Williams College 1820.
Returned to Massachusetts; practiced law at South Hadley. Met and married Mary Ann Dwight here 1834.

Per obituary: 'lawyery at D.H. Lamb & Co.' EH 1909 says 'engaged in soda manufacture' — D.H. Lamb & Co. may have been the soda manufacturing enterprise for which Edward served as legal counsel and commercial partner. Removed from Rochester NY to Cleveland 1835; remained until his death August 1, 1841 at thirty-nine.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-nine; wife outlived him fifty years
narrative_blocks.body: Edward left West Hampton MA as a Williams College graduate, practiced law in Ohio and Massachusetts, then pivoted to soda manufacturing in Cleveland — dying there August 1, 1841 at only thirty-nine, leaving two children under age seven. His wife Mary Ann Dwight survived him until June 4, 1891, outliving him by exactly fifty years.

narrative_blocks.header: Became, late in life, a man of prayer
narrative_blocks.body: The obituary that marked Edward's death at thirty-nine focused less on his law practice at D.H. Lamb & Co. than on the man he had become: late in life he united with the Congregational Church in Ohio City and was remembered by those who knew him simply as 'a man of prayer.' For a direct descendant of Rev. Thomas Hooker — who helped establish the Congregational tradition in Connecticut two centuries earlier — the phrase carried the full weight of inheritance.

narrative_blocks.header: Williams 1820; youngest son of a man who
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Hooker graduated Williams College in 1820 and practiced law at Ravenna Ohio, then South Hadley Massachusetts, before settling in Cleveland with D.H. Lamb & Co. He was the youngest son of Dr. William Hooker of Westhampton, who lived to ninety-four — Edward died at thirty-nine.

parents.father_research_notes: Elihu Dwight MD of South Hadley MA per EH 1909 #1415.

institutions.institution_blurb: Graduated Williams College 1855; afterward studied medicine at East Cambridge.

cross_connections.display_label: East Cambridge physician and Massachusetts legislator, his medical mentor

narrative_blocks.header: Died studying medicine; disease contracted in hospital
narrative_blocks.body: After graduating Williams College in 1855, Edward came to East Cambridge to study medicine under his uncle Dr. Anson Hooker — but died April 26, 1858 at twenty-three of disease contracted in hospital service, before completing his training. Six weeks earlier, his first cousin Edward Dwight had been born in the same household, beginning the physician career Edward would not live to pursue.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband died of yellow fever in Indiana, 1873
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Walworth Smith had been a merchant at St. Thomas in the West Indies before settling at Cincinnati and operating a wrecking business on the Ohio River. He died of yellow fever December 2, 1873 at Evansville Indiana, leaving Mary a widow at thirty-five with two young sons.

burialCemetery.name: Center Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Westhampton MA. Primary burial ground for the William Hooker / Henry Hooker / Kingsley / Drury / Bartlett branch. FindAGrave Cemetery #2198549.
